review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for questions about RL success stories, sim-to-real transfer, and the limitations of model-free RL. The contrasts between locomotion and manipulation are verbally explicit.

question_bank.question: What does the Breakout tunnel example illustrate about reinforcement learning agents?
choices: They can discover strong strategies that are not obvious at first
They can only copy human demonstrations exactly
They always maximize reward safely in the real world
question_bank.explanation: Extended trial and error can uncover strategies that were not manually specified, including ones that look clever or surprising to humans.

question_bank.question: Why is sim-to-real transfer especially encouraging in locomotion?
choices: Because locomotion policies never need contact dynamics
Because even imperfect but randomized simulations can still transfer useful behaviors to the real world
Because real robots are easier to optimize than simulators
Because locomotion rewards are always dense and differentiable
question_bank.explanation: The segment argues that for locomotion, enough variation in simulation can make learned behaviors robust enough to survive mismatch at deployment time.

question_bank.question: What is the main limitation of model-free RL emphasized at the end of this segment?
choices: It can never achieve superhuman game performance
It only works when demonstrations are available
It requires huge interaction, raises safety concerns, and is hard to interpret
It is incompatible with neural networks
question_bank.explanation: Model-free RL can be powerful, but it often pays for that power with large data requirements, unsafe real-world exploration, and limited interpretability when things go wrong.
